messages.content: a thing you've kept
My grandmother's cream cable-knit sweater. First cool morning of October every year. She has been gone twenty-one years and arrives in the wool.
a memory from your house growing up
The Yamaha arriving when I was nine. Two delivery men. My dad sat in front of it for an hour with his hands in his lap before he opened it.
first time you knew something
Six. The egg from Mr. Pell's coop, still warm. Living things make heat.
a christmas memory
Twelve. My brother asleep on the couch in his church clothes. My mother wrapping a last present in the den. My dad at the piano playing something I have never asked about.
a smell that takes you somewhere
Wisteria. The vine over the carport at home blooms two and a half weeks every May. The mockingbird in it has nested for four springs running.
a moment you remember exactly
The Maya recruitment. Poetry section at BookPeople. February 2022. She said I have a job that needs a person and you are the person.
how mose came to be yours
March 2022. Alley behind Threshold. He complained at my shoes for ten minutes. I sat down. He climbed in my lap. Decision made.
a saturday at the studio you go back to
Fall 2023. Sufjan low. The kids worked. Three stayed an extra twenty minutes. The hour I keep coming back to.
your favorite jordan moment
His first Saturday. October 2024. Two hours silent in the corner. Then on the way out, can I come back next week.
a poem of yours that arrived
Six months ago. Six AM. The line about the body being a letter I keep mailing to the same address. I wrote it on my arm in the shower.
your father playing
We drove to the Adolphus when I was eleven. He saw us in the audience. Did not change a thing in his playing. That was the point.
a hospital memory
Houston, 2021. Will's mother told me stories about him at five years old for an hour in the cafeteria. I did not take notes.
the diner
Two summers in Granbury, sixteen and seventeen. An older man tipped me ten on a six-dollar breakfast. Said I reminded him of his daughter.
something your mother gave you
A Mary Oliver collection. Ninth grade. No note. Inside cover in her handwriting. The book that decided something.
a memory of mose specifically
The first chirp test. Naomi was here. I called him from the next room. He chirped. Naomi said did he just talk to you. I said yes he does that.
first sunset in austin
South First Street bridge. Bats came out from Congress half a mile north. I knew calmly I was not going home.
a maya moment that stuck
She told me last spring that the program update I drafted read like I was trying to convince myself. She was right. I rewrote it.
a lou line that stuck
You write to the line, not at it. Don't lose that.
a hannah moment
She read a chapter I had been struggling with. Sent two notes. Both were the right notes. Five minutes later.
a midnight drive memory
Spring 2024. Eleanor crying about Diane on the phone. I drove three and a half hours to Granbury, arrived at one-thirty. The driving was the only thing to do.
the sweater
Cream. Cable-knit. Cedar smell underneath. My grandmother's. Twenty-one years gone.
something you'll remember forever
My mother going quiet after my grandmother's funeral. I was eight. The quiet had a permanent change in it.
someone you've lost
Will Anderson. 2021. Twenty-five. I drove to Houston three times. The third time he was already mostly gone. I sat with his hand for forty minutes.
the friend who died
Will. UT, two years ahead of me. Cancer. Thirteen months. Houston. I still write him unsent letters. I will probably never stop.
will's mother in the cafeteria
Houston, summer 2021. She told me stories about him at five years old. An hour. I did not take notes. I remember everything she said.
the late-night conversation
Will and me. Late 2018. Coffee on Guadalupe. Whether the worst part of death is the dying or the leaving. He took the leaving. I took the dying. Neither of us had any idea.
the houston drive
Three times. The third time alone. Windows down the whole way back to Austin. I did not trust myself in a sealed car.
the unsent letters
To Will. Some short. Some pages. I never send any. The not-sending is the practice.
a clean ending
May 2025. Sunday afternoon in his living room. Neither of us cried. We both saw the same thing at the same time.
berlin
An email from October 2019. Sam wrote it. I keep it in a folder I don't open often. The not-opening is most of why I keep it.
the heb parking lot
Spring 2025. Marcus tried to have a conversation he should have known not to. The yelling surprised me. I have not seen him since.
the religious studies seminar
Spring 2017. Mysticism. Sam was a year ahead, auditing. The first time I noticed Sam was looking at me and not just at the room.
the lady bird walk in november
2017. Two and a half hours with Sam. Neither of us said what was happening for the whole walk.
the house in november 2017
The third floor walk-up off Speedway. Bathtub drained into the wall. I was glad about all of it.
the walk where you knew
Lady Bird, summer 2018. Alone. The water was gold. I knew I did not have words for what I was feeling and would have words eventually.
a relationship that taught you
Sam taught me what was possible. Marcus taught me what I was doing. Rae taught me that what I was doing could be done differently.
first time you remember music in the house
Nine. Yamaha upright arrived. My dad sat in front of it for an hour with his hands in his lap. Played the next morning, alone, before I was awake.
weird kid stuff
Lay in the road in August on a street with no sidewalks. Felt the heat coming up through my back. Private dare with myself. The smell of tar.
why you tip well
Two summers at the diner in high school. An older regular tipped me ten on a six-dollar breakfast. I'm doing the version of that for the kids working diners now.
the porch in summer
Screened porch on the west side of the brick ranch. My mother kept basil in pots until the squirrels figured out it tasted good.
the dare game in the road
Lying flat. Heat coming up. Ear to the asphalt. Listening for cars. The cars were never close. The dare was with myself.
first piece of music you loved
My dad practicing fingerings on the kitchen table before the piano arrived. The same scales over and over. His right hand walking up something nobody could hear.
first egg you held
Six. Mr. Pell's coop. Still warm. The fact I knew after that: living things make heat.
first funeral
My grandmother. Eight. Methodist cemetery on a hill that wasn't a hill. Cooler with ice melting into a slurry my hand kept dipping into.
first time at the adolphus
Eleven. Family drove to Dallas. Dim bar. He saw us in the audience and did not change anything in his playing.
the screened porch
West side. Wicker chair. Quilt across the back. My mother reads there in the warmer months.
the wisteria over the carport
Blooms two and a half weeks in May. Every year. The mockingbird that nests in it has been there four springs running.
first job
The diner in Granbury. Sixteen. Two summers. The tipping behavior I still have comes from this.
the brick ranch
One story. Three bedrooms. One bath. Den. Small kitchen with the same yellow Formica it's had since I was seven.
first christmas you remember
I was twelve. My brother asleep in church clothes. My mother wrapping a last present. My dad playing something at the piano I never asked about.
a place that taught you something
Edinburgh, October 2017. Junior year semester. Walking along the Water of Leith in the rain. The first time I understood that climate could be a medium.
how friendships start
Mine with my closest started on the floor of a UT dorm in 2015. I said something stupid about Foucault. She laughed in three quick syllables. The friendship was the laugh.
a sunset memory from austin
Summer 2018. Lady Bird Lake. Alone. The water was gold. I remember thinking I did not yet have words for what I was feeling and would have words eventually.
the first apartment
Third floor walk-up off Speedway. Junior or sophomore year. Bathtub drained into the wall. Towels under the wall every shower for nine months.
the python apartment
Sophomore or junior year. Roommate kept a snake in a tank in the kitchen. Cooking pasta while the tank made its fluorescent hum eight feet away.
first time in scotland
October 2017. Junior fall. Edinburgh. Wrote more in those four months than any equivalent stretch since.
first time at lady bird
Fall 2015. Freshman year. Walked the Boardwalk with new friends. Knew the city was going to be home.
the seminar
Religious studies on mysticism. Spring 2017. Where I noticed Sam looking at me and not just at the room.
a dorm memory
Jester West freshman fall. Naomi knocked because my music was leaking. The conversation that followed was three hours.
first edinburgh impression
The rain meant something it does not mean in Texas. Texas rain announces itself. Edinburgh rain is climate.
